---
title: ""
author: "Giorgio Arcara"
date: ""
format:
  beamer:
    theme: metropolis
    keep-tex: false
incremental: false
header-includes:
  \setbeamerfont{title}{size=\large}
  \usepackage{hyperref}
  \setbeamertemplate{footline}[frame number]
knitr:
  opts_chunk:
    echo: false
    warning: false
    message: false
    fig.align: center
---

```{r setup}
library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)

set.seed(123)

# ── Shared palette (Metropolis-inspired) ──────────────────────────────────────
COL_DARK   <- "#23373B"
COL_ACCENT <- "#EB811B"
COL_MID    <- "#14B5D0"
COL_RED    <- "#c0392b"

theme_clean <- function(base = 11) {
  theme_minimal(base_size = base) +
    theme(
      plot.background  = element_rect(fill = "white", colour = NA),
      panel.background = element_rect(fill = "white", colour = NA),
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
      panel.grid.major = element_line(colour = "grey90"),
      axis.title       = element_text(size = base - 1),
      axis.text        = element_text(size = base - 2),
      plot.title       = element_text(size = base, face = "bold"),
      plot.subtitle    = element_text(size = base - 2, colour = "grey45"),
      legend.position  = "none"
    )
}

# ── Simulate normative data ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
N <- 220
df <- data.frame(
  age  = round(runif(N, 20, 80)),
  educ = round(rnorm(N, 12, 3))
)
df$educ  <- pmax(5, pmin(20, df$educ))
df$score <- 60 - 0.28 * df$age + 1.1 * df$educ + rnorm(N, 0, 6)

df$age_bin <- cut(df$age,
                  breaks = c(19, 35, 50, 65, 80),
                  labels = c("20-35", "36-50", "51-65", "66-80"))

# ── Fit models ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
fit_full     <- lm(score ~ age + educ, data = df)
fit_age_only <- lm(score ~ age,        data = df)

df$yhat_age  <- fitted(fit_age_only)
df$resid_age <- residuals(fit_age_only)
df$yhat_full <- fitted(fit_full)
df$resid_full <- residuals(fit_full)
df$z_resid   <- scale(df$resid_full)[, 1]

# Subject to highlight in the single-residual plot
idx <- which(df$resid_age < quantile(df$resid_age, 0.05) &
               df$age > 35 & df$age < 55)[1]
pt  <- df[idx, ]

# ── Build figures ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

# A – single histogram
figA <- ggplot(df, aes(x = score)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 22, fill = COL_MID, colour = "white", alpha = 0.85) +
  labs(title    = "Normative sample",
       subtitle = "All participants pooled together",
       x = "Test score", y = "Count") +
  theme_clean()

# B – multiple histograms by age bin
bin_cols <- c("20-35" = "#4E79A7", "36-50" = "#F28E2B",
              "51-65" = "#59A14F", "66-80" = "#E15759")

figB <- ggplot(df, aes(x = score, fill = age_bin)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 12, colour = "white", alpha = 0.90) +
  facet_wrap(~age_bin, nrow = 2,
             labeller = labeller(age_bin = function(x) paste("Age", x))) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = bin_cols) +
  labs(title    = "Splitting by age group",
       subtitle = "Each bin contains fewer participants",
       x = "Test score", y = "Count") +
  theme_clean() +
  theme(strip.text       = element_text(size = 9, face = "bold"),
        strip.background = element_rect(fill = "grey93", colour = NA),
        panel.spacing    = unit(0.6, "lines"))

# Ca – scatter with regression line 
figCa <- ggplot(df, aes(x = age, y = score)) +
  geom_point(colour = COL_DARK, alpha = 0.35, size = 1.8) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = y ~ x,
              se = TRUE, colour = COL_ACCENT,
              fill = COL_ACCENT, alpha = 0.15, linewidth = 1.2) +
  labs(title    = "Score decreases with age",
       subtitle = "Regression line (education held at mean)",
       x = "Age (years)", y = "Test score") +
  theme_clean()


# Cb – scatter with regression line (one point highlighted)
figCb <- ggplot(df, aes(x = age, y = score)) +
  geom_point(colour = COL_DARK, alpha = 0.35, size = 1.8) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = y ~ x,
              se = TRUE, colour = COL_ACCENT,
              fill = COL_ACCENT, alpha = 0.15, linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(aes(x = pt$age, y = pt$yhat_age),
             colour = COL_ACCENT, size = 3.5, shape = 18) +
geom_point(aes(x = pt$age, y = pt$score),
             colour = COL_RED, size = 3.5) +
  labs(title    = "Score decreases with age",
       subtitle = "Regression line (education held at mean)",
       x = "Age (years)", y = "Test score") +
  theme_clean()

# D – single residual highlighted
figD <- ggplot(df, aes(x = age, y = score)) +
  geom_point(colour = COL_DARK, alpha = 0.25, size = 1.8) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = y ~ x,
              se = FALSE, colour = COL_ACCENT, linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(aes(x = pt$age, y = pt$yhat_age),
             colour = COL_ACCENT, size = 3.5, shape = 18) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = pt$age, xend = pt$age,
                   y = pt$yhat_age, yend = pt$score),
               colour = COL_RED, linewidth = 0.8,
               arrow = arrow(ends = "both", length = unit(0.08, "inches"),
                             type = "closed")) +
  geom_point(aes(x = pt$age, y = pt$score),
             colour = COL_RED, size = 3.5) +
  annotate("text",
           x = pt$age + 2.5, y = (pt$yhat_age + pt$score) / 2.5,
           label = "residual\n(observed - predicted)",
           colour = COL_RED, size = 3, hjust = 0) +
  labs(title    = "The residual for one participant",
       subtitle = "How far is the observed score from what was expected?",
       x = "Age (years)", y = "Test score") +
  theme_clean()

# E – all residuals
figE <- ggplot(df, aes(x = age, y = score)) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = age, xend = age,
                   y = yhat_age, yend = score,
                   colour = resid_age < 0),
               alpha = 0.55, linewidth = 0.55) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("TRUE" = COL_RED, "FALSE" = COL_MID)) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = y ~ x,
              se = FALSE, colour = COL_ACCENT, linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(colour = COL_DARK, alpha = 0.35, size = 1.5) +
  labs(title    = "All residuals",
       subtitle = "Blue = above expected; red = below expected",
       x = "Age (years)", y = "Test score") +
  theme_clean()

# F – distribution of standardised residuals
figF <- ggplot(df, aes(x = z_resid)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)),
                 bins = 24, fill = "grey72", colour = "white") +
  stat_function(fun = dnorm, colour = COL_DARK, linewidth = 1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = -1.5, linetype = "dashed",
             colour = COL_RED, linewidth = 0.9) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = -2, linetype = "dotted",
             colour = COL_RED, linewidth = 0.9) +
  annotate("text", x = -1.5 + 0.08, y = 0.44,
           label = "z = -1.5", colour = COL_RED, hjust = 0, size = 3) +
  annotate("text", x = -2 - 0.8, y = 0.44,
           label = "z = -2", colour = COL_RED, hjust = 0, size = 3) +
  labs(title    = "Distribution of adjusted scores",
       subtitle = "Residuals standardised: mean = 0, SD = 1",
       x = "Standardised residual (z)", y = "Density") +
  theme_clean()
```

```{=latex}
\title{{\fontsize{15pt}{16pt}\selectfont Applied Psychometrics}:\\ studying subclinical psychopathologies\\ \vspace{1.5em} \raggedright 
\emph{8.3 Regression-Based Norms}}
\author{Giorgio Arcara,\\ University of Padua \\ IRCCS San Camillo, Venice}

\titlegraphic{
\vspace*{7cm}
\includegraphics[scale=0.15]{Figures/LogoSanCamilloIRCCS_Unipd_alpha.png}
\begin{center}
\vspace{-2.2em}
\includegraphics[scale=0.15]{Figures/CC_license_3_0.png}
\end{center}
}
\date{2026}
\maketitle
```

## Normative data require a single sample

Classical methods (z-scores, non-parametric percentiles, Crawford's modified $t$-test, etc.) all share the same requirement: a **single, well-defined normative group**.

\pause \vfill

```{r figA, fig.height=2.8, fig.width=4.5,  out.width="80%"}
print(figA)
```

\vspace{-0.6em}
\scriptsize
The normative distribution summarises the entire sample with one mean and one SD.


## The problem: performance varies across people

Test scores are known to depend on demographic variables:

- **Age** (e.g., memory and processing speed decline with age)
- **Education** (e.g., higher education is associated with better performance)
- **Sex** (e.g., some tasks show consistent differences)

\pause \vfill

Pooling everyone together **ignores** these differences.
\pause

A 70-year-old and a 25-year-old could not comparable, yet the same norms would be applied to both.


## A natural fix: split into subgroups

<!--One solution is to build **separate normative distributions** for each subgroup. -->

```{r figB, fig.height=3.5, fig.width=5.0, out.width="70%"}
print(figB)
```

\vspace{-0.6em}
\small
Each age band now has its own distribution.


## A first problem with subgroups: arbitrary binning

How wide should each bin be? Where exactly should cut-offs fall?

\pause \vfill

- Age: 20--40, 41--60, 61--80? \quad Or 20--35, 36--55, 56--75?
- Education: $\leq 8$, $9$--$13$, $\geq 14$ years? Why not $\leq 10$, $11$--$15$, $\geq 16$?

\pause \vfill

**These boundaries are arbitrary** — yet they directly affect whether a patient is classified as impaired or not.

\pause \vfill
\small
A person aged 40 may fall in very different normative groups depending on the cut-off chosen. Adding education and sex creates even more combinations, each with fewer and fewer participants.

## A second problem with subgroups: arbitrary binning

The larger the number of bins, the larger the number of required participants to have solid estimates (see previous lecture)

## Regression-based norms: the key idea

Instead of cutting the sample into bins, we can **model** the relationship between demographic variables and test scores directly.

\pause \vfill

$$\hat{Y} = b_0 + b_1 \cdot \text{Age} + b_2 \cdot \text{Education} + b_3 \cdot \text{Sex} + \ldots$$

\pause \vfill

The model gives a **predicted score** for any demographic profile with no arbitrary boundaries required.

\pause \vfill
\small
The key quantity is not the predicted score itself, but how far each person's observed score deviates from it: the **residual**.


## Modelling the effect of age

```{r figCa, fig.height=3.0, fig.width=5.0}
print(figCa)
```


## Modelling the effect of age

```{r figCb, fig.height=3.0, fig.width=5.0}
print(figCb)
```



<!--

\vspace{-0.6em}
\small
As age increases, test scores tend to decrease. The regression line captures this trend
\emph{continuously}, without cutting age into discrete groups.

-->

## A residual

```{r figD, fig.height=2.7, fig.width=5.0}
print(figD)
```


## What is a residual?

For each person, the **residual** is the difference between what they actually scored and what the model predicted for someone with their demographic profile.

\vspace{-0.6em}
\small
The orange diamond is the \emph{predicted} score; the red dot is the \emph{observed} score.
The residual (red arrow) tells us: \emph{is this person performing better or worse than expected?}


## Residuals for every participant

```{r figE, fig.height=3.0, fig.width=5.0}
print(figE)
```

\vspace{-0.6em}
\scriptsize
Each line is one participant's residual. Blue = above expected; red = below expected.
These \textbf{adjusted scores} are now independent of age (and any other modelled variable).


## The distribution of adjusted scores

Once demographic effects are removed, the residuals form a distribution centred on zero.

```{r figF, fig.height=2.7, fig.width=4.8, out.width="70%"}
print(figF)
```

\vspace{-0.6em}
\small
This distribution is the normative reference for \textbf{adjusted} (residualised) scores.
Typical cut-offs: $z < -1.5$ (dashed) or $z < -2$ (dotted).


## From residuals to norms: several methods

Once adjusted scores are available, different norming strategies can be applied:
\small
\pause

- **z-scores on residuals** — standardise using the SD of the normative residuals; calculate percentiles with familiar cut-offs (e.g., $z < -1.5$, $z < -2$)

\pause

- **Non-parametric percentiles of residuals** — rank the patient's residual against the normative distribution; no distributional assumptions needed

\pause

- **Crawford \& Garthwaite (2006)** — a Bayesian single-case approach applied to the residuals; provides a point estimate and a credible interval for the abnormality of the score

\pause

- **Capitani \& Laiacona (1997)** — percentile-based method with confidence intervals, frequently used in Italian neuropsychology

\pause \vfill
\small
_The logic is always the same: express the patient's score relative to what was expected, then ask how unusual that deviation is._


## What regression-based norms solve
\small
\pause
**No arbitrary binning**: demographic variables are treated continuously
\pause

**Simultaneous adjustment**: age, education, sex are all modelled together
\pause

**Efficient use of data**: the whole normative sample contributes to every estimate
\pause

**Flexible**: non-linear effects (e.g.\ Age$^2$) and interactions can be added (explaining this goes beyond the goal of this course)


## What regression-based norms do \emph{not} solve
\small
\pause

**The model must fit well.** If an important predictor is missing, or the functional form is wrong (e.g., a non-linear effect modelled as linear), residuals will absorb **model error** not just individual differences.

\pause \vfill

**Many models can be fitted.** Different choices of predictors and transformations yield different residuals and therefore different norms for the same patient. There is no universally agreed procedure.

\pause \vfill

**Edge uncertainty.** Predictions are less reliable for demographically extreme individuals (very old, very low education). 

\pause \vfill
\small
\centering
![](Figures/triangle.png){width="5%"} _No method is assumption-free. Knowing the assumptions is the first step to using any method responsibly._
